The constant CC is not fixed by this automorphism. In fact, Haar measure is unique only up to an overall multiplicative constant.999In the c=1c=1 warm-up of Maloney–Witten [2006.04855], the Zamolodchikov metric is written as d​s2=4​d​R2/R2ds^{2}=4\,dR^{2}/R^{2}, while the measure used in the formal average is d​R/(2​R)dR/(2R). If one instead quotes the raw Riemannian volume form associated to that metric, one obtains 2​d​R/R2\,dR/R. These differ only by an overall constant. Since the c=1c=1 average diverges, and since finite-volume Narain averages are usually normalized separately, this constant is a convention rather than physical data. After quotienting by R∼1/RR\sim 1/R, the same measure can be written on the fundamental domain as

d​μC​(R)=C​d​RR,R∈[1,∞).d\mu_{C}(R)=C\,{dR\over R},\qquad R\in[1,\infty). (4.71)

For a TT-duality-invariant integrand this is equivalently obtained from 12​∫0∞C​𝑑R/R\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty}C\,dR/R, with the factor of 12\frac{1}{2} implementing the ℤ2\mathbb{Z}_{2} quotient. The self-dual fixed point should be treated with the usual orbifold stabilizer factor, but it does not affect the continuous integral.

Averaging over topological boundary conditions.

Pairing the physical boundary state with LRL_{R} gives the usual compact-boson partition function

ZR​(τ)=ZLR​(T2;τ)=⟨LR;T2|Ψphys​(T2;τ)⟩=1|η​(τ)|2​∑n,w∈ℤq14​(n/R+w​R)2​q¯14​(n/R−w​R)2,q=e2​π​i​τ.\begin{split}Z_{R}(\tau)=Z_{L_{R}}(T^{2};\tau)&=\langle L_{R};T^{2}|\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(T^{2};\tau)\rangle\\ &={1\over|\eta(\tau)|^{2}}\sum_{n,w\in\mathbb{Z}}q^{{1\over 4}(n/R+wR)^{2}}\bar{q}^{{1\over 4}(n/R-wR)^{2}},~~q=e^{2\pi i\tau}.\end{split} (4.72)

The factor 1/|η​(τ)|21/|\eta(\tau)|^{2} comes from the oscillator part of the fixed physical boundary Hamiltonian (4.23). The discrete sum is supplied by the topological boundary condition LRL_{R}, which restricts the allowed charge sectors to

(α,β)=(nR,w​R),n,w∈ℤ.(\alpha,\beta)=\left({n\over R},wR\right),\qquad n,w\in\mathbb{Z}. (4.73)

Substituting these charge labels into the zero-mode eigenvalue formula (4.25) gives

pL=nR+w​R,pR=nR−w​R.p_{\mathrm{L}}={n\over R}+wR,\qquad p_{\mathrm{R}}={n\over R}-wR. (4.74)

This matches with the fact that the exponents in (4.72) are precisely the eigenvalues of the current-algebra zero-mode contribution to L0L_{0} and L¯0\bar{L}_{0}. Equivalently, the lattice sum in (4.72) is the sum over the local primaries 𝒪n,w\mathcal{O}_{n,w} selected by the topological boundary condition LRL_{R}.

Combining the previous discussion of duality quotient and the integral measure, the topological-boundary average over the compact-boson locus is thus

⟨Z​(τ)⟩top,C(c=1)=∫ℒcomp(1)𝑑μC​(L)​ZL​(T2;τ)=C​∫1∞d​RR​ZR​(τ).\big\langle Z(\tau)\big\rangle_{\mathrm{top},C}^{(c=1)}=\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{comp}}^{(1)}}d\mu_{C}(L)\,Z_{L}(T^{2};\tau)=C\int_{1}^{\infty}{dR\over R}\,Z_{R}(\tau). (4.75)

In the rest of this subsection we set C=1C=1 unless otherwise stated. The integral in (4.75) is over the compact-boson locus only. The degenerate topological boundary conditions L∞L_{\infty} and L0L_{0} are not integrated over as ordinary points of this Haar orbit; rather, they appear as endpoints in a partial compactification of the radius line.

The expression (4.75) is the rank-one analogue of the Narain average in [2006.04855]. Note that it is not a finitely normalized average. Indeed, for R→∞R\to\infty, the winding sectors with w≠0w\neq 0 are exponentially suppressed, and the w=0w=0 sector gives

ZR​(τ)∼1|η​(τ)|2​∑n∈ℤexp⁡(−π​τ2​n2R2).Z_{R}(\tau)\sim{1\over|\eta(\tau)|^{2}}\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}\exp\left(-{\pi\tau_{2}n^{2}\over R^{2}}\right). (4.76)

By Poisson resummation,

∑n∈ℤexp⁡(−π​τ2​n2R2)∼Rτ2,R→∞.\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}\exp\left(-{\pi\tau_{2}n^{2}\over R^{2}}\right)\sim{R\over\sqrt{\tau_{2}}},\qquad R\to\infty. (4.77)

Thus

ZR​(τ)∼Rτ2​|η​(τ)|2,R→∞,Z_{R}(\tau)\sim{R\over\sqrt{\tau_{2}}\,|\eta(\tau)|^{2}},\qquad R\to\infty, (4.78)

and hence

∫∞d​RR​ZR​(τ)∼1τ2​|η​(τ)|2​∫∞𝑑R=∞.\int^{\infty}{dR\over R}\,Z_{R}(\tau)\sim{1\over\sqrt{\tau_{2}}\,|\eta(\tau)|^{2}}\int^{\infty}dR=\infty. (4.79)

The rank-one average therefore has no normalized probabilistic interpretation. From the present SymTFT viewpoint, this divergence has a simple structural origin: the space of topological boundary conditions as the radius line is noncompact, and its large-radius end approaches the degenerate topological boundary condition L∞=ℝ⊕0L_{\infty}=\mathbb{R}\oplus 0, which describes the noncompact boson. The dual endpoint L0=0⊕ℝL_{0}=0\oplus\mathbb{R} is obtained by applying TT-duality. This reintreprets the c=1c=1 pathology of Narain averaging as an endpoint effect in the space of topological boundary conditions.

This example is nevertheless useful because it makes the general mechanism explicit. The compact-boson radius is not inserted as a coupling in the local physical boundary condition, nor in the boundary Hamiltonian (4.23). It is supplied by the choice of a topological boundary condition LRL_{R} of the fixed ℝ\mathbb{R}-valued BF SymTFT. Averaging over radii is therefore identified with integrating over topological boundary conditions of the SymTFT, where the duality quotient and the integral measure both enjoy a SymTFT interpretation. For higher Narain rank, the same construction leads to the Narain moduli space reproduced by the space topological boundary conditions, and the invariant integral measure becomes the Haar-induced measure on the Narain quotient by dualities.

4.2 c>1c>1

We now pass from the rank-one compact boson to higher-rank Narain theories [2006.04855, 2006.04839]. Throughout this subsection, cc denotes the rank of the Narain lattice, equivalently the dimension of the target torus TcT^{c}, i.e., cL=cR=cc_{\mathrm{L}}=c_{\mathrm{R}}=c. We use the ℝ\mathbb{R}-valued BF SymTFT introduced at the beginning of this section, with action

SBF=12​π​∫X3∑i=1cai∧d​bi,ai,bi∈Ω1​(X3;ℝ),S_{\mathrm{BF}}={1\over 2\pi}\int_{X_{3}}\sum_{i=1}^{c}a_{i}\wedge db_{i},\qquad a_{i},b_{i}\in\Omega^{1}(X_{3};\mathbb{R}), (4.80)

and line operators

Uα→​[γ]=exp⁡(i​αi​∮γai),Vβ→​[γ]=exp⁡(i​βi​∮γbi),α→,β→∈ℝc.U_{\vec{\alpha}}[\gamma]=\exp\left(i\alpha_{i}\oint_{\gamma}a_{i}\right),\qquad V_{\vec{\beta}}[\gamma]=\exp\left(i\beta_{i}\oint_{\gamma}b_{i}\right),\qquad\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}\in\mathbb{R}^{c}. (4.81)

The defect group reads

𝔻c=ℝc⊕ℝc,\mathbb{D}_{c}=\mathbb{R}^{c}\oplus\mathbb{R}^{c},

with the Dirac pairing

⟨(α→,β→),(α→′,β→′)⟩=α→⋅β→′+α→′⋅β→mod​ℤ.\big\langle(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}),(\vec{\alpha}^{\prime},\vec{\beta}^{\prime})\big\rangle=\vec{\alpha}\cdot\vec{\beta}^{\prime}+\vec{\alpha}^{\prime}\cdot\vec{\beta}\quad\mathrm{mod}\ \mathbb{Z}. (4.82)

We also use the quadratic refinement

q​(α→,β→)=α→⋅β→mod​ℤ,q(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})=\vec{\alpha}\cdot\vec{\beta}\quad\mathrm{mod}\ \mathbb{Z}, (4.83)

which controls the spin of the corresponding line.

Physical boundary.

The physical boundary condition is the fixed conformal boundary condition at the physical end of the BF slab. It prepares the state

|Ψphys​(Σ;Ω)⟩∈ℋBF​(Σ).|\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(\Sigma;\Omega)\rangle\in\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{BF}}(\Sigma). (4.84)

Introduce the left- and right-moving combinations

AL,i=ai+bi,AR,i=ai−bi,i=1,…,c.A_{\mathrm{L},i}=a_{i}+b_{i},\qquad A_{\mathrm{R},i}=a_{i}-b_{i},\qquad i=1,\ldots,c. (4.85)

On the physical boundary Σ\Sigma, with local complex coordinates z,z¯z,\bar{z}, the conformal boundary condition is

(AL,i)z¯|Σ=0,(AR,i)z|Σ=0,i=1,…,c.(A_{\mathrm{L},i})_{\bar{z}}\big|_{\Sigma}=0,\qquad(A_{\mathrm{R},i})_{z}\big|_{\Sigma}=0,\qquad i=1,\ldots,c. (4.86)

Equivalently,

(ai+bi)z¯|Σ=0,(ai−bi)z|Σ=0.(a_{i}+b_{i})_{\bar{z}}\big|_{\Sigma}=0,\qquad(a_{i}-b_{i})_{z}\big|_{\Sigma}=0. (4.87)

Thus the physical boundary supplies cc left-moving and cc right-moving abelian current algebras.

On the Lorentzian boundary cylinder Σ=Sφ1×ℝt\Sigma=S^{1}_{\varphi}\times\mathbb{R}_{t}, the same boundary condition can be written as

(ai)t|Σ=(bi)φ|Σ,(bi)t|Σ=(ai)φ|Σ.(a_{i})_{t}\big|_{\Sigma}=(b_{i})_{\varphi}\big|_{\Sigma},\qquad(b_{i})_{t}\big|_{\Sigma}=(a_{i})_{\varphi}\big|_{\Sigma}. (4.88)

The corresponding physical boundary Hamiltonian is fixed once and for all by this conformal boundary condition:

Hphys=14​π​∫S1𝑑φ:∑i=1c((ai)φ2+(bi)φ2):,H_{\mathrm{phys}}={1\over 4\pi}\int_{S^{1}}d\varphi\,:\!\sum_{i=1}^{c}\left((a_{i})_{\varphi}^{2}+(b_{i})_{\varphi}^{2}\right)\!:, (4.89)

and the spatial momentum is

Pphys=12​π​∫S1𝑑φ:∑i=1c(ai)φ​(bi)φ:.P_{\mathrm{phys}}={1\over 2\pi}\int_{S^{1}}d\varphi\,:\!\sum_{i=1}^{c}(a_{i})_{\varphi}(b_{i})_{\varphi}\!:. (4.90)

Let 𝖯L,i\mathsf{P}_{\mathrm{L},i} and 𝖯R,i\mathsf{P}_{\mathrm{R},i} denote the zero-mode charge operators of the left- and right-moving current algebras. A bulk line labelled by (α→,β→)∈𝔻c(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})\in\mathbb{D}_{c} specifies a charge sector of the physical boundary theory. Here α→\vec{\alpha} and β→\vec{\beta} are real charge labels, not dynamical fields. In the charge sector labelled by (α→,β→)(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}), the zero-mode operators have eigenvalues

p→L​(α→,β→)=α→+β→,p→R​(α→,β→)=α→−β→.\vec{p}_{\mathrm{L}}(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})=\vec{\alpha}+\vec{\beta},\qquad\vec{p}_{\mathrm{R}}(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})=\vec{\alpha}-\vec{\beta}. (4.91)

The Virasoro zero-mode operators are

L0=14​|𝖯L|2+NL,L¯0=14​|𝖯R|2+NR.L_{0}={1\over 4}\left|\mathsf{P}_{\mathrm{L}}\right|^{2}+N_{\mathrm{L}},\qquad\bar{L}_{0}={1\over 4}\left|\mathsf{P}_{\mathrm{R}}\right|^{2}+N_{\mathrm{R}}. (4.92)

Therefore, in the charge sector (α→,β→)(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}), their eigenvalues are

hα→,β→;NL=14​|α→+β→|2+NL,h¯α→,β→;NR=14​|α→−β→|2+NR.h_{\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta};N_{\mathrm{L}}}={1\over 4}\left|\vec{\alpha}+\vec{\beta}\right|^{2}+N_{\mathrm{L}},\qquad\bar{h}_{\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta};N_{\mathrm{R}}}={1\over 4}\left|\vec{\alpha}-\vec{\beta}\right|^{2}+N_{\mathrm{R}}. (4.93)

Thus

Hphys=L0+L¯0−c12,Pphys=L0−L¯0.H_{\mathrm{phys}}=L_{0}+\bar{L}_{0}-{c\over 12},\qquad P_{\mathrm{phys}}=L_{0}-\bar{L}_{0}. (4.94)

The Narain moduli do not appear in this local physical boundary Hamiltonian. They enter only through the choice of topological cap.

Continuous compact topological boundary conditions.

A compact topological boundary condition is a discrete Lagrangian subgroup

L⊂𝔻c.L\subset\mathbb{D}_{c}. (4.95)

More explicitly, LL is associated with a rank-2​c2c lattice satisfying

q​(ℓ)∈ℤ,⟨ℓ,ℓ′⟩∈ℤ,ℓ,ℓ′∈L,q(\ell)\in\mathbb{Z},\qquad\big\langle\ell,\ell^{\prime}\big\rangle\in\mathbb{Z},\qquad\ell,\ell^{\prime}\in L, (4.96)

together with the maximality condition

L≅L⟂,L⟂:={x∈𝔻c:⟨x,ℓ⟩∈ℤ​for​all​ℓ∈L}.L\cong L^{\perp},\qquad L^{\perp}:=\left\{x\in\mathbb{D}_{c}:\big\langle x,\ell\big\rangle\in\mathbb{Z}\ \mathrm{for\ all}\ \ell\in L\right\}. (4.97)

The condition q​(ℓ)∈ℤq(\ell)\in\mathbb{Z} says that the lines ending on the topological boundary are bosonic101010Since we want the resulting absolute theories being bosonic CFTs instead of their fermionization.. The condition ⟨ℓ,ℓ′⟩∈ℤ\langle\ell,\ell^{\prime}\rangle\in\mathbb{Z} says that all lines within LL have mutually trivial braiding. The maximality condition says that every line trivially-braiding with those in LL is already in LL. In other words, this maximal isotropic condition tells us the lattice associated to LL is even self-dual in ℝc,c\mathbb{R}^{c,c}, but in the SymTFT language written in the (α→,β→)(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}) polarization determined by the BF line operators.

We now parameterize these compact topological boundary conditions explicitly from the SymTFT. Start from a compact boundary as the reference boundary condition

L∘={(n→,w→):n→,w→∈ℤc}⊂𝔻c=ℝc⊕ℝc.L_{\circ}=\left\{(\vec{n},\vec{w}):\vec{n},\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c}\right\}\subset\mathbb{D}_{c}=\mathbb{R}^{c}\oplus\mathbb{R}^{c}. (4.98)

The pairing on L∘L_{\circ} is

⟨(n→,w→),(n→′,w→′)⟩=n→⋅w→′+n→′⋅w→∈ℤ,\big\langle(\vec{n},\vec{w}),(\vec{n}^{\prime},\vec{w}^{\prime})\big\rangle=\vec{n}\cdot\vec{w}^{\prime}+\vec{n}^{\prime}\cdot\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}, (4.99)

and the quadratic refinement is

q​(n→,w→)=n→⋅w→∈ℤ.q(\vec{n},\vec{w})=\vec{n}\cdot\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}. (4.100)

Conversely, if x=(α→,β→)∈𝔻cx=(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})\in\mathbb{D}_{c} pairs integrally with every (n→,w→)∈L∘(\vec{n},\vec{w})\in L_{\circ}, then

α→⋅w→+β→⋅n→∈ℤ∀n→,w→∈ℤc.\vec{\alpha}\cdot\vec{w}+\vec{\beta}\cdot\vec{n}\in\mathbb{Z}\qquad\forall\,\vec{n},\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c}. (4.101)

Taking w→=0\vec{w}=0 implies β→∈ℤc\vec{\beta}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c}, and taking n→=0\vec{n}=0 implies α→∈ℤc\vec{\alpha}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c}. Hence x∈L∘x\in L_{\circ}, so

L∘=L∘⟂.L_{\circ}=L_{\circ}^{\perp}. (4.102)

Therefore, L∘L_{\circ} is a compact Lagrangian boundary condition of the BF SymTFT. In fact, it is the higher-rank analogue of the self-dual-radius boundary at R=1R=1 in the c=1c=1 example.

In order to find other topological boundary conditions from this reference one, we determine the relevant automorphism group of the BF theory. Introduce the 2​c2c-component vector of gauge fields

𝒜=(a1⋮acb1⋮bc),η=(0𝟏c𝟏c0).\mathcal{A}=\begin{pmatrix}a_{1}\\ \vdots\\ a_{c}\\ b_{1}\\ \vdots\\ b_{c}\end{pmatrix},\qquad\eta=\begin{pmatrix}0&\mathbf{1}_{c}\\ \mathbf{1}_{c}&0\end{pmatrix}. (4.103)

Up to a total derivative, the BF action can be written in the following symmetric form

SBF=14​π​∫X3𝒜T​η​𝑑𝒜.S_{\mathrm{BF}}={1\over 4\pi}\int_{X_{3}}\mathcal{A}^{T}\eta\,d\mathcal{A}. (4.104)

Indeed,

14​π​∫𝒜T​η​𝑑𝒜=14​π​∫∑i=1c(ai∧d​bi+bi∧d​ai)=12​π​∫∑i=1cai∧d​bi{1\over 4\pi}\int\mathcal{A}^{T}\eta\,d\mathcal{A}={1\over 4\pi}\int\sum_{i=1}^{c}\left(a_{i}\wedge db_{i}+b_{i}\wedge da_{i}\right)={1\over 2\pi}\int\sum_{i=1}^{c}a_{i}\wedge db_{i} (4.105)

on a closed three-manifold, or modulo the boundary polarization on a slab.

Now consider a constant linear transformation of variables

𝒜⟼N​𝒜,N∈G​L​(2​c,ℝ).\mathcal{A}\longmapsto N\mathcal{A},\qquad N\in GL(2c,\mathbb{R}). (4.106)

The action becomes

SBF⟼14​π​∫𝒜T​NT​η​N​𝑑𝒜.S_{\mathrm{BF}}\longmapsto{1\over 4\pi}\int\mathcal{A}^{T}N^{T}\eta N\,d\mathcal{A}. (4.107)

This field redefinition is an automorphism of the BF SymTFT precisely when

NT​η​N=η.N^{T}\eta N=\eta. (4.108)

Therefore, the bulk SymTFT heory has a continuous group of linear automorphisms

O​(c,c;ℝ)={N∈G​L​(2​c,ℝ):NT​η​N=η}.O(c,c;\mathbb{R})=\left\{N\in GL(2c,\mathbb{R}):N^{T}\eta N=\eta\right\}. (4.109)

This is a statement about the automorphisms of the ℝ\mathbb{R}-valued BF theory, or equivalently about invertible topological interfaces (i.e. 0-form symmetry operators) of the SymTFT. It is not yet a quotient by physical equivalences of the absolute boundary CFT.

Let us now see the same group directly from the line operators. For a line labelled by

x=(α→,β→)∈𝔻c=ℝc⊕ℝc,x=(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})\in\mathbb{D}_{c}=\mathbb{R}^{c}\oplus\mathbb{R}^{c}, (4.110)

write

𝒲x​[γ]:=Uα→​[γ]​Vβ→​[γ]=exp⁡(i​∮γxT​𝒜).\mathcal{W}_{x}[\gamma]:=U_{\vec{\alpha}}[\gamma]V_{\vec{\beta}}[\gamma]=\exp\left(i\oint_{\gamma}x^{T}\mathcal{A}\right). (4.111)

The Dirac pairing can be written as

⟨x,y⟩=xT​η​ymod​ℤ.\langle x,y\rangle=x^{T}\eta y\quad\mathrm{mod}\ \mathbb{Z}. (4.112)

Equivalently, the braiding phase is

𝒲x​[γ1]​𝒲y​[γ2]=exp⁡(2​π​i​xT​η​y​Link​(γ1,γ2))​𝒲y​[γ2]​𝒲x​[γ1].\mathcal{W}_{x}[\gamma_{1}]\mathcal{W}_{y}[\gamma_{2}]=\exp\left(2\pi i\,x^{T}\eta y\,\mathrm{Link}(\gamma_{1},\gamma_{2})\right)\mathcal{W}_{y}[\gamma_{2}]\mathcal{W}_{x}[\gamma_{1}]. (4.113)

A general field transformation 𝒜↦N​𝒜\mathcal{A}\mapsto N\mathcal{A} sends the line label to

x⟼NT​x.x\longmapsto N^{T}x. (4.114)

Given N∈O​(c,c;ℝ)N\in O(c,c;\mathbb{R}), this preserves the Dirac pairing / line braiding:

⟨NT​x,NT​y⟩=xT​N​η​NT​y=xT​η​y=⟨x,y⟩.\langle N^{T}x,N^{T}y\rangle=x^{T}N\eta N^{T}y=x^{T}\eta y=\langle x,y\rangle. (4.115)

Equivalently, we may describe the induced automorphism directly on the defect group by

x⟼g​x,g∈O​(c,c;ℝ),gT​η​g=η.x\longmapsto gx,\qquad g\in O(c,c;\mathbb{R}),\qquad g^{T}\eta g=\eta. (4.116)

Note that we haven not used the Narain CFT as an input to obtain this O​(c,c;ℝ)O(c,c;\mathbb{R}); it is the group of linear automorphisms of the SymTFT defect data preserving the braiding.

Now choose the reference compact topological boundary condition

L∘={(n→,w→):n→,w→∈ℤc}⊂𝔻c.L_{\circ}=\left\{(\vec{n},\vec{w}):\vec{n},\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c}\right\}\subset\mathbb{D}_{c}. (4.117)

For any g∈O​(c,c;ℝ)g\in O(c,c;\mathbb{R}) we can define

Lg=g​L∘.L_{g}=gL_{\circ}. (4.118)

This is again a compact Lagrangian topological boundary condition. To see this, let ℓ1,ℓ2∈L∘\ell_{1},\ell_{2}\in L_{\circ}, then the braiding-preserving tells us

⟨g​ℓ1,g​ℓ2⟩=⟨ℓ1,ℓ2⟩∈ℤ,\langle g\ell_{1},g\ell_{2}\rangle=\langle\ell_{1},\ell_{2}\rangle\in\mathbb{Z}, (4.119)

and similarly

q​(g​ℓ)=q​(ℓ)∈ℤ.q(g\ell)=q(\ell)\in\mathbb{Z}. (4.120)

Moreover,

(g​L∘)⟂=g​(L∘⟂)=g​L∘.(gL_{\circ})^{\perp}=g(L_{\circ}^{\perp})=gL_{\circ}. (4.121)

Thus acting with the SymTFT automorphism group on the reference Lagrangian boundary produces another allowed topological cap. From a defect perspective, this amounts to pushing invertible topological surfaces labeled by O​(c,c;ℝ)O(c,c;\mathbb{R}) elements onto the reference topological boundary L∘L_{\circ} and transform it to g​L∘gL_{\circ}. This is the intrinsic SymTFT origin of the continuous family of compact topological boundary conditions, which correspond to Narain moduli before the quotient.

As in c=1c=1, where the modulus RR labeling different CFTs can enter the parameterization of topological boundary conditions explicitly, we now present how the usual torus sigma-model moduli, metric and BB-field, can enter the space of topological boundary conditions. Without any input from Narain CFTs, we set

G=GT>0,BT=−B,G=G^{T}>0,\qquad B^{T}=-B, (4.122)

where GG is a positive definite symmetric matrix and BB is an antisymmetric matrix. We choose the positive square root of GG,

e=G1/2,eT=e,e2=G,e=G^{1/2},\qquad e^{T}=e,\qquad e^{2}=G, (4.123)

and then define a linear map on the defect group 𝔻c=ℝc⊕ℝc\mathbb{D}_{c}=\mathbb{R}^{c}\oplus\mathbb{R}^{c} by

gG,B=(e−Te−T​B0e).g_{G,B}=\begin{pmatrix}e^{-T}&e^{-T}B\\ 0&e\end{pmatrix}. (4.124)

More explicitly, on a line defect labeled by x=(α→,β→)x=(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}), gG,Bg_{G,B} acts as

gG,B:(α→β→)⟼(e−T​(α→+B​β→)e​β→).g_{G,B}:\begin{pmatrix}\vec{\alpha}\\ \vec{\beta}\end{pmatrix}\longmapsto\begin{pmatrix}e^{-T}(\vec{\alpha}+B\vec{\beta})\\ e\vec{\beta}\end{pmatrix}. (4.125)

It is easy to check gG,Bg_{G,B} satisfies

gG,BT​η​gG,B=(e−10BT​e−1eT)​(0ee−Te−T​B)=(0𝟏c𝟏cBT+B)=(0𝟏c𝟏c0)=η,\begin{split}g_{G,B}^{T}\eta g_{G,B}&=\begin{pmatrix}e^{-1}&0\\ B^{T}e^{-1}&e^{T}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}0&e\\ e^{-T}&e^{-T}B\end{pmatrix}\\ &=\begin{pmatrix}0&\mathbf{1}_{c}\\ \mathbf{1}_{c}&B^{T}+B\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}0&\mathbf{1}_{c}\\ \mathbf{1}_{c}&0\end{pmatrix}=\eta,\end{split} (4.126)

where we used BT=−BB^{T}=-B. Therefore gG,Bg_{G,B} parametrizes the O​(c,c;ℝ)O(c,c;\mathbb{R}) automorphism of the SymTFT defect data.

Applying this defect automorphism to the reference boundary L∘L_{\circ} gives an explicit expression of the topological boundary condition

LG,B={(e−T​(n→+B​w→),e​w→):n→,w→∈ℤc}⊂𝔻c.L_{G,B}=\left\{\left(e^{-T}(\vec{n}+B\vec{w}),e\vec{w}\right):\vec{n},\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c}\right\}\subset\mathbb{D}_{c}. (4.127)

That is to say, GG and BB appear as coordinates on the SymTFT family of compact topological boundary conditions. Note that the above derivation requires no input from the physical boundary Hamiltonian. Rather, we are just characterizing which SymTFT line defects (α→,β→)(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}) are allowed to end on the topological boundary, and the characterization happens to match the Narain moduli GG and BB.

Although the above discussion is sufficient to see LG,BL_{G,B} generated by gG,Bg_{G,B} is topological boundary condition, let us still check the Lagrangian condition explicitly. Write an element in the defect group as

ℓG,B​(n→,w→)=(α→G,B​(n→,w→),β→G,B​(n→,w→))=(e−T​(n→+B​w→),e​w→).\ell_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})=\left(\vec{\alpha}_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w}),\vec{\beta}_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})\right)=\left(e^{-T}(\vec{n}+B\vec{w}),e\vec{w}\right). (4.128)

For two such elements,

ℓ=ℓG,B​(n→,w→),ℓ′=ℓG,B​(n→′,w→′),\ell=\ell_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w}),\qquad\ell^{\prime}=\ell_{G,B}(\vec{n}^{\prime},\vec{w}^{\prime}),

the Dirac pairing is

⟨ℓ,ℓ′⟩=α→G,B​(n→,w→)⋅β→G,B​(n→′,w→′)+α→G,B​(n→′,w→′)⋅β→G,B​(n→,w→)=(n→+B​w→)⋅w→′+(n→′+B​w→′)⋅w→=n→⋅w→′+n→′⋅w→∈ℤ.\begin{split}\langle\ell,\ell^{\prime}\rangle&=\vec{\alpha}_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})\cdot\vec{\beta}_{G,B}(\vec{n}^{\prime},\vec{w}^{\prime})+\vec{\alpha}_{G,B}(\vec{n}^{\prime},\vec{w}^{\prime})\cdot\vec{\beta}_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})\\ &=(\vec{n}+B\vec{w})\cdot\vec{w}^{\prime}+(\vec{n}^{\prime}+B\vec{w}^{\prime})\cdot\vec{w}\\ &=\vec{n}\cdot\vec{w}^{\prime}+\vec{n}^{\prime}\cdot\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}.\end{split} (4.129)

The BB-dependent terms cancel because BB is antisymmetric:

(B​w→)⋅w→′+(B​w→′)⋅w→=w→T​BT​w→′+w→′⁣T​BT​w→=−w→T​B​w→′−w→′⁣T​B​w→=0.(B\vec{w})\cdot\vec{w}^{\prime}+(B\vec{w}^{\prime})\cdot\vec{w}=\vec{w}^{T}B^{T}\vec{w}^{\prime}+\vec{w}^{\prime T}B^{T}\vec{w}=-\vec{w}^{T}B\vec{w}^{\prime}-\vec{w}^{\prime T}B\vec{w}=0.

Similarly,

q​(ℓ)=α→G,B​(n→,w→)⋅β→G,B​(n→,w→)=(n→+B​w→)⋅w→=n→⋅w→∈ℤ,q(\ell)=\vec{\alpha}_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})\cdot\vec{\beta}_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})=(\vec{n}+B\vec{w})\cdot\vec{w}=\vec{n}\cdot\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}, (4.130)

because w→T​B​w→=0\vec{w}^{T}B\vec{w}=0. Thus the lines in LG,BL_{G,B} are bosonic and have mutually trivial braiding. Finally, since gG,Bg_{G,B} preserves the pairing,

LG,B⟂=(gG,B​L∘)⟂=gG,B​(L∘⟂)=gG,B​L∘=LG,B.L_{G,B}^{\perp}=(g_{G,B}L_{\circ})^{\perp}=g_{G,B}(L_{\circ}^{\perp})=g_{G,B}L_{\circ}=L_{G,B}. (4.131)

Therefore LG,BL_{G,B} is a compact Lagrangian topological boundary condition.

Another quick consistency check is to consider c=1c=1, where we have

G=R2,B=0,e=R,G=R^{2},\qquad B=0,\qquad e=R, (4.132)

and thus

LG,B={(nR,w​R):n,w∈ℤ}=LR,L_{G,B}=\left\{\left({n\over R},wR\right):n,w\in\mathbb{Z}\right\}=L_{R}, (4.133)

which exactly reduces to the RR-labeled topological boundary conditions.

Local primaries and surviving symmetry lines.

We now explain the operator content of the absolute 2D theory obtained via a topological boundary condition LL. The elements of LL are precisely the bulk line labels that become trivial on the topological boundary. Thus, for each ℓ=(α→,β→)∈L\ell=(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})\in L, the composite bulk line

𝒲ℓ​[γ]:=Uα→​[γ]​Vβ→​[γ]\mathcal{W}_{\ell}[\gamma]:=U_{\vec{\alpha}}[\gamma]V_{\vec{\beta}}[\gamma] (4.134)

can end on the topological boundary. If we bring the other endpoint of this line to the physical boundary, it creates a local operator of the absolute 2D theory. We denote this local operator by 𝒪ℓ\mathcal{O}_{\ell} . In this sense, the Lagrangian subgroup LL gives the current-algebra primary spectrum of the absolute CFT.

The conformal weights of 𝒪ℓ\mathcal{O}_{\ell} are read using the fixed physical boundary condition. Namely, the zero-mode eigenvalues in the charge sector ℓ=(α→,β→)\ell=(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta}) are

p→L​(ℓ)=α→+β→,p→R​(ℓ)=α→−β→.\vec{p}_{\mathrm{L}}(\ell)=\vec{\alpha}+\vec{\beta},\qquad\vec{p}_{\mathrm{R}}(\ell)=\vec{\alpha}-\vec{\beta}. (4.135)

Therefore, before adding oscillator descendants,

hℓ=14​|p→L​(ℓ)|2,h¯ℓ=14​|p→R​(ℓ)|2.h_{\ell}={1\over 4}\left|\vec{p}_{\mathrm{L}}(\ell)\right|^{2},\qquad\bar{h}_{\ell}={1\over 4}\left|\vec{p}_{\mathrm{R}}(\ell)\right|^{2}. (4.136)

Moreover,

hℓ−h¯ℓ=α→⋅β→=q​(ℓ)∈ℤ,h_{\ell}-\bar{h}_{\ell}=\vec{\alpha}\cdot\vec{\beta}=q(\ell)\in\mathbb{Z}, (4.137)

so the endpoint operator has integer spin in the bosonic 2D theory. This is the operator-theoretic meaning of the condition q​(ℓ)∈ℤq(\ell)\in\mathbb{Z}.

For the boundary condition LG,BL_{G,B}, parameterized by GG and BB , the condensed lines are labelled by n→,w→∈ℤc\vec{n},\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c} through

ℓG,B​(n→,w→)=(e−T​(n→+B​w→),e​w→)∈LG,B.\ell_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})=\left(e^{-T}(\vec{n}+B\vec{w}),e\vec{w}\right)\in L_{G,B}. (4.138)

The corresponding local primary is denoted by 𝒪n→,w→G,B\mathcal{O}_{\vec{n},\vec{w}}^{G,B}. Its left- and right-moving momenta are

p→L​(n→,w→;G,B)=e−T​(n→+(B+G)​w→),p→R​(n→,w→;G,B)=e−T​(n→+(B−G)​w→).\vec{p}_{\mathrm{L}}(\vec{n},\vec{w};G,B)=e^{-T}\bigl(\vec{n}+(B+G)\vec{w}\bigr),\qquad\vec{p}_{\mathrm{R}}(\vec{n},\vec{w};G,B)=e^{-T}\bigl(\vec{n}+(B-G)\vec{w}\bigr). (4.139)

and the corresponding conformal weights are

hn→,w→G,B=14​|e−T​(n→+(B+G)​w→)|2,h¯n→,w→G,B=14​|e−T​(n→+(B−G)​w→)|2,h_{\vec{n},\vec{w}}^{G,B}={1\over 4}\left|e^{-T}\bigl(\vec{n}+(B+G)\vec{w}\bigr)\right|^{2},\qquad\bar{h}_{\vec{n},\vec{w}}^{G,B}={1\over 4}\left|e^{-T}\bigl(\vec{n}+(B-G)\vec{w}\bigr)\right|^{2}, (4.140)

That is to say, the familiar Narain primary labeled by momentum and winding quantum numbers (n→,w→)(\vec{n},\vec{w}) is now realized from the SymTFT line 𝒲ℓG,B​(n→,w→)\mathcal{W}_{\ell_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})}.

We now discuss the topological symmetry lines of the absolute theory. A general bulk line labelled by

γ=(ρ→,σ→)∈𝔻c\gamma=(\vec{\rho},\vec{\sigma})\in\mathbb{D}_{c} (4.141)

can be laid on the topological boundary. However, two such lines define the same boundary topological defect if they differ by a line that is trivialized by the boundary condition LL. Therefore the surviving topological line operators are labelled by 𝔻c/L\mathbb{D}_{c}/L [2401.10165]. Their action on the local primaries is determined by the braiding of bulk lines. Namely,

γ:𝒪ℓ⟼exp(2πi⟨γ,ℓ⟩)𝒪ℓ.\gamma:\quad\mathcal{O}_{\ell}\longmapsto\exp\!\left(2\pi i\,\big\langle\gamma,\ell\big\rangle\right)\mathcal{O}_{\ell}. (4.142)

This action is well-defined on the quotient 𝔻c/L\mathbb{D}_{c}/L, because shifting γ\gamma by an element of LL changes the phase by an integer multiple of 2​π​i2\pi i.

For L=LG,BL=L_{G,B}, the quotient 𝔻c/LG,B\mathbb{D}_{c}/L_{G,B} is a 2​c2c-torus. A convenient representative of its elements is

γϑ→m,ϑ→w=(e−T​(ϑ→w+B​ϑ→m),e​ϑ→m),ϑ→m,ϑ→w∈ℝc/ℤc.\gamma_{\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}},\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}}=\left(e^{-T}\bigl(\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}+B\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}\bigr),e\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}\right),\qquad\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}},\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}\in\mathbb{R}^{c}/\mathbb{Z}^{c}. (4.143)

The periodicity follows because shifting

ϑ→m↦ϑ→m+r→,ϑ→w↦ϑ→w+s→,r→,s→∈ℤc,\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}\mapsto\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}+\vec{r},\qquad\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}\mapsto\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}+\vec{s},\qquad\vec{r},\vec{s}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c},

changes γϑ→m,ϑ→w\gamma_{\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}},\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}} by

(e−T​(s→+B​r→),e​r→)=ℓG,B​(s→,r→)∈LG,B.\left(e^{-T}(\vec{s}+B\vec{r}),e\vec{r}\right)=\ell_{G,B}(\vec{s},\vec{r})\in L_{G,B}.

Therefore ϑ→m\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}} and ϑ→w\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}} indeed parametrize two cc-dimensional compact symmetry groups.

The action of this surviving line on the primary 𝒪n→,w→G,B\mathcal{O}_{\vec{n},\vec{w}}^{G,B} is

⟨γϑ→m,ϑ→w,ℓG,B​(n→,w→)⟩=(ϑ→w+B​ϑ→m)⋅w→+(n→+B​w→)⋅ϑ→m=ϑ→w⋅w→+ϑ→m⋅n→mod​ℤ,\begin{split}\big\langle\gamma_{\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}},\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}},\ell_{G,B}(\vec{n},\vec{w})\big\rangle&=\bigl(\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}+B\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}\bigr)\cdot\vec{w}+\bigl(\vec{n}+B\vec{w}\bigr)\cdot\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}\\ &=\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}\cdot\vec{w}+\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}\cdot\vec{n}\quad\mathrm{mod}\ \mathbb{Z},\end{split} (4.144)

where the BB-dependent terms cancel because BT=−BB^{T}=-B. Hence

γϑ→m,ϑ→w:𝒪n→,w→G,B⟼exp[2πi(ϑ→m⋅n→+ϑ→w⋅w→)]𝒪n→,w→G,B.\gamma_{\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}},\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}}:\quad\mathcal{O}_{\vec{n},\vec{w}}^{G,B}\longmapsto\exp\!\left[2\pi i\left(\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}\cdot\vec{n}+\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}\cdot\vec{w}\right)\right]\mathcal{O}_{\vec{n},\vec{w}}^{G,B}. (4.145)

Thus 𝒪n→,w→G,B\mathcal{O}_{\vec{n},\vec{w}}^{G,B} carries charges

(Qm,Qw)=(n→,w→)(Q_{\mathrm{m}},Q_{\mathrm{w}})=(\vec{n},\vec{w}) (4.146)

under

U​(1)mc×U​(1)wc.U(1)_{\mathrm{m}}^{\,c}\times U(1)_{\mathrm{w}}^{\,c}. (4.147)

Here U​(1)mcU(1)_{\mathrm{m}}^{\,c} is generated by the parameter ϑ→m\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{m}}, and measures momentum charge n→\vec{n}, while U​(1)wcU(1)_{\mathrm{w}}^{\,c} is generated by ϑ→w\vec{\vartheta}_{\mathrm{w}}, and measures winding charge w→\vec{w}.

Noncompact topological boundary conditions.

The compact Narain boundary conditions LG,BL_{G,B} are not the only maximal isotropic subgroups of 𝔻c\mathbb{D}_{c}. They are the ones relevant for compact torus CFTs, since they associate with discrete rank-2​c2c lattices. The enlarged space of topological boundary conditions also contains degenerate maximal isotropic subgroups which are continuous. The simplest examples are

L∞(c)=ℝc⊕0,L0(c)=0⊕ℝc.L_{\infty}^{(c)}=\mathbb{R}^{c}\oplus 0,\qquad L_{0}^{(c)}=0\oplus\mathbb{R}^{c}. (4.148)

Similarly to c=1c=1 case, it is straightforward to check they are Lagrangian. Physically, L∞(c)L_{\infty}^{(c)} describes cc noncompact bosons, while L0(c)L_{0}^{(c)} describes their dual noncompact bosons. They are the higher-rank analogues of the R=∞R=\infty and R=0R=0 endpoints in the c=1c=1 discussion.

More generally, one may decompactify only part of the compact Lagrangian boundary condition. After choosing a splitting

𝔻c=𝔻r⊕𝔻c−r,𝔻r=ℝr⊕ℝr,\mathbb{D}_{c}=\mathbb{D}_{r}\oplus\mathbb{D}_{c-r},\qquad\mathbb{D}_{r}=\mathbb{R}^{r}\oplus\mathbb{R}^{r}, (4.149)

one can consider boundary conditions of the form

L∞,r;G⟂,B⟂=(ℝr⊕0)⊕LG⟂,B⟂,L_{\infty,r;G_{\perp},B_{\perp}}=\bigl(\mathbb{R}^{r}\oplus 0\bigr)\oplus L_{G_{\perp},B_{\perp}}, (4.150)

or

L0,r;G⟂,B⟂=(0⊕ℝr)⊕LG⟂,B⟂.L_{0,r;G_{\perp},B_{\perp}}=\bigl(0\oplus\mathbb{R}^{r}\bigr)\oplus L_{G_{\perp},B_{\perp}}. (4.151)

Here LG⟂,B⟂⊂𝔻c−rL_{G_{\perp},B_{\perp}}\subset\mathbb{D}_{c-r} is an ordinary compact Narain boundary condition for the remaining c−rc-r compact directions. Thus L∞,r;G⟂,B⟂L_{\infty,r;G_{\perp},B_{\perp}} describes rr noncompact bosons together with a compact Narain theory of rank c−rc-r, while L0,r;G⟂,B⟂L_{0,r;G_{\perp},B_{\perp}} describes the corresponding dual noncompact directions.

These noncompact boundary conditions arise as degeneration of compact Narain topological boundaries. For example, take B=0B=0 and

G=diag​(R12,…,Rr2,G⟂).G=\mathrm{diag}\bigl(R_{1}^{2},\ldots,R_{r}^{2},G_{\perp}\bigr). (4.152)

Then the compact boundary condition contains charge labels

(n1R1,…,nrRr;w1​R1,…,wr​Rr)\left({n_{1}\over R_{1}},\ldots,{n_{r}\over R_{r}};w_{1}R_{1},\ldots,w_{r}R_{r}\right) (4.153)

in the first rr directions. Sending R1,…,Rr→∞R_{1},\ldots,R_{r}\to\infty, the momentum labels ni/Rin_{i}/R_{i} become continuous while the winding sectors with wi≠0w_{i}\neq 0 are pushed to infinite energy in the physical boundary Hamiltonian. The limiting topological boundary condition is thus of the form (4.150). Sending Ri→0R_{i}\to 0 instead gives the dual boundary condition (4.151).

The compact Narain average considered below integrates only over the compact Narain locus of discrete lattices LG,BL_{G,B}. The degenerate boundary conditions (4.148)–(4.151) are not ordinary points of the space of Lagrangian boundaries whose integral measure for averaging will be shown to be Haar measure as in c=1c=1 case. Rather, they should be regarded as boundary or cusp loci in a partial compactification of the topological-boundary space. Their role is nevertheless important for understanding convergence: divergences of Narain averages arise precisely from regions of the compact Narain locus that approach such noncompact limits.

Dualities and integral measure.

Having determined the parametrization of the (compact) topological boundary conditions is given by O​(c,c;ℝ)O(c,c;\mathbb{R}), the next natural question to ask is what is the genuine space we want to average over, and whether that matches the Narain moduli space. This requires us to identify redundancies in this parametrization. First, the reference Lagrangian lattice L∘≃ℤc⊕ℤcL_{\circ}\simeq\mathbb{Z}^{c}\oplus\mathbb{Z}^{c} has an automorphism group,

O(c,c;ℤ)=Aut(L∘,⟨,⟩,q).O(c,c;\mathbb{Z})=\mathrm{Aut}(L_{\circ},\langle\,,\,\rangle,q). (4.154)

Acting on the integer labels (n→,w→)(\vec{n},\vec{w}), this group preserves the integral Dirac pairing and the quadratic refinement associated to spins. We thus have

g​γ​L∘=g​L∘,γ∈O​(c,c;ℤ),g\gamma L_{\circ}=gL_{\circ},\qquad\gamma\in O(c,c;\mathbb{Z}), (4.155)

which means O​(c,c;ℤ)O(c,c;\mathbb{Z}) is a redundancy in the parametrization of the same compact topological boundary condition. In the absolute 2D CFT, this is the familiar integral T-duality group. It includes changes of integral torus basis, integral BB-field shifts, and factorized dualities.

There is also a continuous frame redundancy associated with the fixed physical boundary condition111111Note that this redundancy is due to this specific physical boundary, but not SymTFT itself. That is to say, given a SymTFT, different physical boundaries may require different redundancy of space of topological boundary conditions.. Recall that the Hamiltonian supported on the physical boundary only depends on the Euclidean norms

|p→L|2,|p→R|2.|\vec{p}_{\mathrm{L}}|^{2},\qquad|\vec{p}_{\mathrm{R}}|^{2}. (4.156)

Therefore independent orthogonal rotations

p→L⟼OL​p→L,p→R⟼OR​p→R,OL,OR∈O​(c),\vec{p}_{\mathrm{L}}\longmapsto O_{\mathrm{L}}\vec{p}_{\mathrm{L}},\qquad\vec{p}_{\mathrm{R}}\longmapsto O_{\mathrm{R}}\vec{p}_{\mathrm{R}},\qquad O_{\mathrm{L}},O_{\mathrm{R}}\in O(c), (4.157)

only change the orthonormal frame of the left- and right-moving current algebras. They do not change the absolute CFT. Thus, after fixing the physical boundary condition, the compact component of the space of topological boundary conditions is

ℒNarain(c)=O​(c,c;ℤ)\O​(c,c;ℝ)/(O​(c)×O​(c)).\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}=O(c,c;\mathbb{Z})\backslash O(c,c;\mathbb{R})/\bigl(O(c)\times O(c)\bigr). (4.158)

Equivalently, with the convention Lg=g​L∘L_{g}=gL_{\circ}, one may first quotient on the right by the automorphisms of L∘L_{\circ} and then quotient by the left/right frame rotations; the two descriptions are related by g↦g−1g\mapsto g^{-1}. This is exactly the usual Narain moduli space, now obtained as the space of compact topological boundary conditions of the fixed ℝ\mathbb{R}-valued BF SymTFT.

Before performing the averaging, let us decide the measure from the same SymTFT automorphism principle. Since O​(c,c;ℝ)O(c,c;\mathbb{R}) is the continuous automorphism group preserving the BF braiding data, the natural measure on the orbit of compact Lagrangian boundaries is the measure induced from Haar measure on O​(c,c;ℝ)O(c,c;\mathbb{R}). Descending this measure through the discrete quotient by O​(c,c;ℤ)O(c,c;\mathbb{Z}) and the frame quotient by O​(c)×O​(c)O(c)\times O(c) gives an invariant measure on ℒNarain(c)\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}. We denote it by

d​μ​(L).d\mu(L). (4.159)

This is the continuous version of the groupoid measure discussed in Section 2.2: the finite groupoid factor 1/|Aut​(L)|1/|\mathrm{Aut}(L)| is replaced by the Haar measure on the quotient of the continuous automorphism orbit.

The overall normalization of d​μ​(L)d\mu(L) is conventional. In a G,BG,B coordinate patch, the Haar-induced measure can be written, up to an overall constant, as

d​μ​(G,B)=CNar​∏1≤i≤j≤cd​Gi​j​∏1≤i<j≤cd​Bi​j(detG)c.d\mu(G,B)=C_{\mathrm{Nar}}\,{\displaystyle\prod_{1\leq i\leq j\leq c}dG_{ij}\prod_{1\leq i<j\leq c}dB_{ij}\over(\det G)^{c}}. (4.160)

For c=1c=1, this reduces to d​G/G=2​d​R/RdG/G=2\,dR/R, which agrees with the radius Haar measure of the previous subsection up to the expected overall normalization.

This Haar-induced measure is the same measure that appears in the standard Narain average. From the 2D CFT point of view, the Narain moduli space carries the Zamolodchikov metric, and its associated volume form agrees with the locally homogeneous measure on

O​(c,c;ℤ)\O​(c,c;ℝ)/(O​(c)×O​(c)).O(c,c;\mathbb{Z})\backslash O(c,c;\mathbb{R})/(O(c)\times O(c)).

From the SymTFT viewpoint, the same measure is obtained because the space of compact topological caps is an O​(c,c;ℝ)O(c,c;\mathbb{R})-orbit and the averaging measure must be invariant under this defect-data automorphism group. Thus the SymTFT construction reproduces both the Narain moduli space [Narain:1985jj, Narain:1986am] and the natural Narain averaging measure [2006.04839, 2006.04855].

For c>1c>1, the Haar volume of the compact Narain quotient is finite. Hence one may use the normalized measure

d​μ^​(L)=d​μ​(L)Vol​(ℒNarain(c)).d\widehat{\mu}(L)={d\mu(L)\over\mathrm{Vol}\!\left(\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}\right)}. (4.161)

The compact Narain average below is taken with respect to this measure, whenever the observable being averaged is integrable.

Averaging over topological-boundary conditions.

Pairing the fixed physical boundary state with a compact topological boundary condition LL gives the Narain partition function

ZL​(T2;τ)=⟨L;T2|Ψphys​(T2;τ)⟩.Z_{L}(T^{2};\tau)=\langle L;T^{2}|\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(T^{2};\tau)\rangle. (4.162)

Using the Lagrangian lattice L⊂𝔻cL\subset\mathbb{D}_{c}, this is

ZL​(τ)=1|η​(τ)|2​c​ΘL​(τ),Z_{L}(\tau)={1\over|\eta(\tau)|^{2c}}\Theta_{L}(\tau), (4.163)

where

ΘL​(τ)=∑ℓ=(α→,β→)∈Lq14​|α→+β→|2​q¯14​|α→−β→|2,q=e2​π​i​τ.\Theta_{L}(\tau)=\sum_{\ell=(\vec{\alpha},\vec{\beta})\in L}q^{{1\over 4}\left|\vec{\alpha}+\vec{\beta}\right|^{2}}\bar{q}^{{1\over 4}\left|\vec{\alpha}-\vec{\beta}\right|^{2}},\qquad q=e^{2\pi i\tau}. (4.164)

The factor 1/|η​(τ)|2​c1/|\eta(\tau)|^{2c} comes from the oscillator part of the fixed physical boundary Hamiltonian (4.89). The theta function ΘL​(τ)\Theta_{L}(\tau) is supplied entirely by the topological boundary condition LL. This aligns with our previous discussion that changing LL changes the allowed current-algebra primary sectors, while the local physical boundary dynamics is held fixed.

For the explicit compact topological boundary condition LG,BL_{G,B}, this becomes

ZG,B​(τ)=1|η​(τ)|2​c​∑n→,w→∈ℤcq14​|e−T​(n→+(B+G)​w→)|2​q¯14​|e−T​(n→+(B−G)​w→)|2.Z_{G,B}(\tau)={1\over|\eta(\tau)|^{2c}}\sum_{\vec{n},\vec{w}\in\mathbb{Z}^{c}}q^{{1\over 4}\left|e^{-T}\bigl(\vec{n}+(B+G)\vec{w}\bigr)\right|^{2}}\bar{q}^{{1\over 4}\left|e^{-T}\bigl(\vec{n}+(B-G)\vec{w}\bigr)\right|^{2}}. (4.165)

This is the standard genus-one partition function of the toroidal Narain compactification with metric GG and BB-field BB [2006.04855]. In the present SymTFT interpretation, however, (G,B)(G,B) are not couplings in the physical boundary Hamiltonian. They are coordinates on the space of topological boundaries LG,BL_{G,B}, specifying which bulk line-defect labels are allowed to end on the topological boundary.

The topological-boundary average over compact Narain caps is therefore

⟨Z​(τ)⟩top(c)=∫ℒNarain(c)𝑑μ^​(L)​ZL​(T2;τ),\big\langle Z(\tau)\big\rangle_{\mathrm{top}}^{(c)}=\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}}d\widehat{\mu}(L)\,Z_{L}(T^{2};\tau), (4.166)

whenever the integral converges as an ordinary integral. Equivalently, using the pairing notation of (4.162),

⟨Z​(τ)⟩top(c)=∫ℒNarain(c)𝑑μ^​(L)​⟨L;T2|Ψphys​(T2;τ)⟩.\big\langle Z(\tau)\big\rangle_{\mathrm{top}}^{(c)}=\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}}d\widehat{\mu}(L)\,\langle L;T^{2}|\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(T^{2};\tau)\rangle. (4.167)

Here d​μ^​(L)d\widehat{\mu}(L) is the normalized Haar-induced measure introduced in (4.161). If one instead works with the unnormalized Haar measure d​μ​(L)d\mu(L), then (4.166) should be replaced by

1Vol​(ℒNarain(c))​∫ℒNarain(c)𝑑μ​(L)​ZL​(T2;τ).{1\over\mathrm{Vol}\!\left(\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}\right)}\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}}d\mu(L)\,Z_{L}(T^{2};\tau).

The compact Narain average integrates only over the compact locus of discrete rank-2​c2c lattices LL. The degenerate noncompact endpoints discussed above are not included as ordinary points of the Haar integral; they appear as cusp or boundary loci approached by sequences of compact caps.

Since the oscillator factor is independent of LL, the average reduces to the Haar average of the Siegel–Narain theta function:

⟨Z​(τ)⟩top(c)=1|η​(τ)|2​c​∫ℒNarain(c)𝑑μ^​(L)​ΘL​(τ).\big\langle Z(\tau)\big\rangle_{\mathrm{top}}^{(c)}={1\over|\eta(\tau)|^{2c}}\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}}d\widehat{\mu}(L)\,\Theta_{L}(\tau). (4.168)

This is the higher-rank version of the c=1c=1 radius average.

There is an important distinction between the finite volume of the compact Narain quotient and the convergence of the partition-function integral. For c>1c>1, the Haar volume of ℒNarain(c)\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)} is finite, so the normalized measure d​μ^​(L)d\widehat{\mu}(L) exists. However, the genus-one theta integral

∫ℒNarain(c)𝑑μ^​(L)​ΘL​(τ)\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}}d\widehat{\mu}(L)\,\Theta_{L}(\tau)

is finite only for c>2c>2 [2006.04855]. At c=2c=2, the moduli-space volume is finite, but the theta integral still diverges at the cusps, for example in large-volume and large-complex-structure limits. Thus c=2c=2 is closer to the c=1c=1 example from the viewpoint of the unregularized genus-one partition-function average, even though the quotient itself has finite Haar volume.

For c>2c>2, the normalized genus-one average is finite. In the convention where d​μ^​(L)d\widehat{\mu}(L) has unit total volume, the Siegel–Weil formula gives [2006.04855]

∫ℒNarain(c)𝑑μ^​(L)​ΘL​(τ)=Ec/2​(τ)τ2c/2,c>2,\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Narain}}^{(c)}}d\widehat{\mu}(L)\,\Theta_{L}(\tau)={E_{c/2}(\tau)\over\tau_{2}^{c/2}},\qquad c>2, (4.169)

up to the convention used for the normalization of the real-analytic Eisenstein series Ec/2​(τ)E_{c/2}(\tau). Therefore

⟨Z​(τ)⟩top(c)=Ec/2​(τ)τ2c/2​|η​(τ)|2​c,c>2.\big\langle Z(\tau)\big\rangle_{\mathrm{top}}^{(c)}={E_{c/2}(\tau)\over\tau_{2}^{c/2}|\eta(\tau)|^{2c}},\qquad c>2. (4.170)

The measure in this statement is the Haar-induced measure on the Narain quotient, or equivalently the measure obtained from the Zamolodchikov metric on the Narain moduli space. The Siegel–Weil formula is the identity that evaluates the corresponding Haar average of the Siegel–Narain theta function; it is not a separate choice of measure.

This completes the higher-rank version of the c=1c=1 dictionary. The fixed physical boundary condition supplies the current algebra, oscillator descendants, and Hamiltonian. The compact topological boundary condition LL supplies the even self-dual charge lattice, hence the current-algebra primary spectrum. Averaging over Narain CFTs is therefore realized as averaging over compact topological boundary conditions of a fixed ℝ\mathbb{R}-valued BF SymTFT.

5 Toward JT Gravity and 3D Gravity

In this paper we have studied low-dimensional ensemble averaging holography from the perspective of SymTFTs. The central idea is that the physical boundary condition is held fixed, while the topological boundary condition is varied. In the closed Marolf–Maxfield model this becomes a groupoid sum over finite-set caps, whereas in the Narain example it becomes an integral over (compact) topological boundary conditions of an ℝ\mathbb{R}-valued BF SymTFT. These two examples suggest that some familiar holographic ensemble averages can be reinterpreted as averages over topological completions of a fixed relative theory. It would be interesting to understand how far this interpretation extends beyond the topological and abelian examples discussed above.

5.1 Toward JT Gravity and Random Matrix Theory

A natural next example is JT gravity and its relation to random matrix theory [1903.11115, 1907.03363]. Although JT gravity is usually presented as a metric-dilaton theory, it also has a first-order BF-theory formulation [1812.00918, 1905.02726]. In its simplest bosonic form, one may write schematically121212We thank Patrick Jefferson for valuable discussions inspiring the content of this subsection.

SBF=i​∫YTr​Φ​FAS_{\mathrm{BF}}=i\int_{Y}{\mathrm{Tr}}\,\Phi F_{A} (5.1)

together with the Euler-characteristic term S0​χ​(Y)S_{0}\chi(Y) and the appropriate asymptotic boundary term. Here AA is an S​L​(2,ℝ)SL(2,\mathbb{R}) connection, and Φ\Phi is an adjoint-valued scalar field whose components include the JT dilaton. Integrating over Φ\Phi imposes the flatness of AA, so the bulk path integral reduces, up to the boundary degrees of freedom, to an integral over a moduli space of flat S​L​(2,ℝ)SL(2,\mathbb{R}) connections. The asymptotic A​d​S2AdS_{2} boundary condition is not topological; it gives the Schwarzian boundary mode and hence the usual one-dimensional quantum-mechanical observable Tr​e−β​H\mathrm{Tr}\,e^{-\beta H}.

From the BF-theory point of view, the scalar field Φ\Phi is a local zero-form field in the 2D topological theory. Therefore given gauge-invariant functions of Φ\Phi, for example

𝒪f​(x)=f​(C​(x)),C​(x)=12​Tr​Φ​(x)2,{\cal O}_{f}(x)=f\!\left(C(x)\right),\qquad C(x)=\frac{1}{2}{\mathrm{Tr}}\,\Phi(x)^{2}, (5.2)

one defines local operators of the underlying 2D TFT. In the JT boundary problem, the quadratic Casimir CC is naturally related, up to normalization conventions, to the Hamiltonian of the Schwarzian quantum mechanics. Thus insertions of functions of the BF scalar should be viewed as topological representatives of spectral observables of the boundary quantum mechanics.

This makes JT gravity structurally close to the Marolf–Maxfield example discussed in Section 3. In that example, a fixed topological parent SymTFT was capped by a topological boundary condition labelled by a finite set SS, and the resulting absolute theory had

ZS​(S1)=|S|.Z_{S}(S^{1})=|S|. (5.3)

Averaging over the finite-set caps then produced the Marolf–Maxfield moments. For JT gravity, the analogous SymTFT would be the S​L​(2,ℝ)SL(2,\mathbb{R}) BF theory, refined by the local algebra generated by the dilaton/BF scalar and by the non-topological asymptotic JT boundary condition. Given there are nontrivial local operators in the 2D bulk, the physical boundary should prepare a class of relative one-dimensional quantum mechanics [2411.14997] rather than a single quantum mechanics or number. A topological boundary condition LL would then specify an absolute completion of this relative boundary theory, namely a Hilbert space and a Hamiltonian on which the Schwarzian/spectral observables act.

In this language, one expects a fixed topological boundary condition LL to produce a factorizing one-dimensional quantum mechanics,

ZL​(β)=⟨L;S1|Ψphys​(β)⟩=TrℋL​e−β​HL.Z_{L}(\beta)=\big\langle L;S^{1}\,\big|\,\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(\beta)\big\rangle={\mathrm{Tr}}_{\mathcal{H}_{L}}e^{-\beta H_{L}}. (5.4)

For several asymptotic boundaries, the same topological cap should give the corresponding product of traces in the fixed absolute theory. Averaging over topological boundary conditions would therefore take the schematic form

⟨∏i=1nZ​(βi)⟩top=∫ℒJT𝑑μ​(L)​⟨L;⨆i=1nSi1|Ψphys​(β1,…,βn)⟩.\left\langle\prod_{i=1}^{n}Z(\beta_{i})\right\rangle_{\mathrm{top}}=\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{JT}}}d\mu(L)\,\big\langle L;\bigsqcup_{i=1}^{n}S^{1}_{i}\,\big|\,\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(\beta_{1},\ldots,\beta_{n})\big\rangle. (5.5)

The desired statement is that, for an appropriate space ℒJT\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{JT}} of admissible topological boundary conditions and an appropriate measure d​μ​(L)d\mu(L), this average reproduces the double-scaled random matrix ensemble average

⟨∏i=1nTr​e−βi​H⟩RMT.\left\langle\prod_{i=1}^{n}{\mathrm{Tr}}\,e^{-\beta_{i}H}\right\rangle_{\mathrm{RMT}}. (5.6)

Different choices of additional topological data, such as orientation reversal, spin or pin structures, and supersymmetry, should then correspond to different random matrix universality classes [1907.03363].

We emphasize that the above perspective should be regarded as a very sloppy proposal. The main missing ingredients are a precise classification of topological boundary conditions for the noncompact S​L​(2,ℝ)SL(2,\mathbb{R}) BF theory relevant to JT gravity, and a derivation of the measure on this space that reproduces the matrix-model eigenvalue measure and spectral curve. Nevertheless, the analogy with Section 3 suggests a useful organizing principle.

5.2 Toward Virasoro TFT and 3D Gravity

Another natural direction is pure 3D gravity with negative cosmological constant. Recent work suggests that the fixed-topology path integral of pure AdS3\mathrm{AdS}_{3} gravity can be reformulated in terms of a topological theory built from the quantization of Teichmüller space, usually called Virasoro T(Q)FT [2304.13650, 2401.13900]. In particular, for suitable quasi-local boundary conditions, fixed-length amplitudes can be written as Virasoro T(Q)FT amplitude-squared, while fixed-angle amplitudes are naturally related to Conformal Turaev–Viro theory [2507.12696]. More precisely, Virasoro T(Q)FT should be regarded as a chiral building block. The gravitational answer is obtained by combining a left-moving and a right-moving copy, schematically

𝒯grav∼𝒯Vir⊠𝒯¯Vir,\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{grav}}\sim\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{Vir}}\boxtimes\overline{\mathcal{T}}_{\mathrm{Vir}}, (5.7)

or, at the level of fixed-topology amplitudes, by an absolute-square type pairing of Virasoro-T(Q)FT wavefunctions. This is the non-abelian analogue of the abelian Chern–Simons/BF structures discussed in Section 4.

It is useful to phrase this point in the language of SymTFT. An ordinary rational-CFT SymTFT based on a modular tensor category (MTC) usually starts from a chosen extended chiral algebra 𝒜\mathcal{A}. The corresponding MTC captures topological defect lines that commute with, or preserve, this full chiral algebra. By contrast, Virasoro T(Q)FT should be thought of as the universal version in which only the Virasoro symmetry is fixed. Its topological line operators are expected to encode defects that commute with the left and right Virasoro algebras, without requiring preservation of any further extended chiral algebra. This is why it is the natural candidate SymTFT for an ensemble in which the central charge and Virasoro kinematics are fixed, while the extra chiral algebra, the spectrum of Virasoro primaries, and the OPE data are supplied by the topological boundary.

Let Σ\Sigma be the conformal boundary. The Virasoro T(Q)FT assigns to Σ\Sigma a Hilbert space of Virasoro conformal blocks, which we denote schematically by

ℋVir​(Σ).\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{Vir}}(\Sigma). (5.8)

For gravity one should use the left-right doubled space

ℋgrav​(Σ)=ℋVir​(Σ)⊗ℋ¯Vir​(Σ).\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{grav}}(\Sigma)=\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{Vir}}(\Sigma)\otimes\overline{\mathcal{H}}_{\mathrm{Vir}}(\Sigma). (5.9)

This is the direct analogue of the Hilbert space of the ℝ\mathbb{R}-valued BF SymTFT in the Narain example. A physical conformal boundary condition, with complex structure, operator insertions, and possible moduli collectively denoted by Ω\Omega, should prepare a relative state

|Ψphys​(Σ;Ω)⟩∈ℋgrav​(Σ).\big|\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(\Sigma;\Omega)\big\rangle\in\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{grav}}(\Sigma). (5.10)

This boundary is not topological. It contains the Virasoro Hamiltonian. For example, on the torus one expects the physical boundary to carry

Hphys=L0+L¯0−c12,H_{\mathrm{phys}}=L_{0}+\overline{L}_{0}-\frac{c}{12}, (5.11)

so that, after choosing an absolute completion via a topological boundary, the torus observable has the usual form

ZL​(τ,τ¯)=TrℋL​(qL0−c/24​q¯L¯0−c/24).Z_{L}(\tau,\overline{\tau})=\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathcal{H}_{L}}\left(q^{L_{0}-c/24}\,\overline{q}^{\,\overline{L}_{0}-c/24}\right). (5.12)

Here LL denotes a topological boundary condition of the doubled Virasoro T(Q)FT. As in the previous examples, the topological boundary is required to carry zero Hamiltonian. It does not change the local Virasoro time evolution; rather, it specifies the absolute Hilbert space ℋL\mathcal{H}_{L} on which this Hamiltonian acts.

This is very close in spirit to the Narain construction. In Section 4, the physical boundary supplied the oscillator sector, the current algebra descendants, and the Hamiltonian, while the (compact) topological boundary condition supplied the even self-dual charge lattice and hence the current-algebra primary spectrum. In the Virasoro case, the physical boundary should supply the universal Virasoro kinematics: the conformal blocks, the descendant contributions, and the conformal Hamiltonian. The topological boundary condition should supply the dynamical CFT data: the spectrum of Virasoro primaries, their multiplicities, and the OPE coefficients obeying crossing and modular invariance.

For example, on a torus one may write schematically

ZL​(τ,τ¯)=∑(h,h¯)∈𝒮LNL​(h,h¯)​χh​(τ)​χ¯h¯​(τ¯),Z_{L}(\tau,\overline{\tau})=\sum_{(h,\overline{h})\in\mathcal{S}_{L}}N_{L}(h,\overline{h})\,\chi_{h}(\tau)\,\overline{\chi}_{\overline{h}}(\overline{\tau}), (5.13)

where the characters are universal Virasoro objects, while the spectrum 𝒮L\mathcal{S}_{L} and multiplicities NL​(h,h¯)N_{L}(h,\overline{h}) are supplied by the Lagrangian boundary LL. Similarly, for a four-point function on the sphere, one expects an expansion of the schematic form

⟨𝒪1​𝒪2​𝒪3​𝒪4⟩L=∑p,p¯C12​p(L)​C34​p(L)​ℱp​[h1h2h3h4]​(z)​ℱ¯p¯​[h¯1h¯2h¯3h¯4]​(z¯).\big\langle\mathcal{O}_{1}\mathcal{O}_{2}\mathcal{O}_{3}\mathcal{O}_{4}\big\rangle_{L}=\sum_{p,\overline{p}}C^{(L)}_{12p}\,C^{(L)}_{34p}\,\mathcal{F}_{p}\left[\begin{matrix}h_{1}&h_{2}\\ h_{3}&h_{4}\end{matrix}\right](z)\,\overline{\mathcal{F}}_{\overline{p}}\left[\begin{matrix}\overline{h}_{1}&\overline{h}_{2}\\ \overline{h}_{3}&\overline{h}_{4}\end{matrix}\right](\overline{z}). (5.14)

Again, the conformal blocks are universal and belong to the physical boundary data, while the coefficients Ci​j​k(L)C^{(L)}_{ijk} and the spectrum of exchanged primaries are part of the topological completion.

Thus a possible SymTFT interpretation of the ensemble dual to pure AdS3\mathrm{AdS}_{3} gravity is the following. We conjecture that there should exist a space

ℒVir\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Vir}} (5.15)

of admissible topological boundary conditions of the doubled Virasoro T(Q)FT. For each L∈ℒVirL\in\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Vir}}, the pairing

ZL​(Σ;m)=⟨L;Σ|Ψphys​(Σ;m)⟩Z_{L}(\Sigma;m)=\big\langle L;\Sigma\,\big|\,\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(\Sigma;m)\big\rangle (5.16)

defines an absolute 2D CFT-like object with fixed central charge and fixed Virasoro symmetry. The proposed ensemble average is then

⟨Z​(Σ;m)⟩top=∫ℒVir𝑑μ​(L)​⟨L;Σ|Ψphys​(Σ;m)⟩.\big\langle Z(\Sigma;m)\big\rangle_{\mathrm{top}}=\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Vir}}}d\mu(L)\,\big\langle L;\Sigma\,\big|\,\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(\Sigma;m)\big\rangle. (5.17)

For several disconnected conformal boundaries, the same topological boundary condition should be used on all components:

⟨∏a=1nZ​(Σa;ma)⟩top=∫ℒVir𝑑μ​(L)​∏a=1n⟨L;Σa|Ψphys​(Σa;ma)⟩.\left\langle\prod_{a=1}^{n}Z(\Sigma_{a};m_{a})\right\rangle_{\mathrm{top}}=\int_{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Vir}}}d\mu(L)\,\prod_{a=1}^{n}\big\langle L;\Sigma_{a}\,\big|\,\Psi_{\mathrm{phys}}(\Sigma_{a};m_{a})\big\rangle. (5.18)

At fixed LL, the answer factorizes, as it should for a single absolute CFT. The non-factorization of the gravitational answer is then produced by the integral over topological caps.

This perspective also gives a natural way to interpret the wormhole computations in Virasoro T(Q)FT. A multi-boundary Euclidean wormhole computes a moment of CFT data in the putative ensemble. In the present language, this moment should arise because the same topological boundary condition LL is used to cap all physical boundaries before being averaged over. Thus the Virasoro-T(Q)FT wormhole is not changing the local boundary Hamiltonian; it is correlating the absolute completion data, namely the spectrum and OPE coefficients, across different copies of the physical boundary.

Here the main missing ingredient, which is also the most nontrivial step, is the correct definition of ℒVir\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Vir}} and of the measure d​μ​(L)d\mu(L). For finite rational T(Q)FTs, topological boundary conditions are described by Lagrangian algebras in a modular tensor category. For the RR-valued BF theories used in the Narain example, this becomes a continuous space of Lagrangian subgroups equipped with a Haar-type measure. The Virasoro case should be simultaneously non-rational and non-compact analogue of these two situations. A topological boundary condition should be an object that pairs left and right Virasoro conformal blocks into a single-valued, crossing-symmetric, modular invariant full CFT. In categorical language, it should be a kind of Lagrangian algebra or full-center object in the non-rational braided category of Virasoro representations, but this statement requires functional-analytic input beyond the finite semisimple framework.

The relation to quantum groups may be useful precisely here. Quantum Teichmüller theory is closely related to the modular double of Uq​(𝔰​𝔩​(2,ℝ))U_{q}(\mathfrak{sl}(2,\mathbb{R})) [1109.6295, 1302.3454], with

q=ei​π​b2,q~=ei​π​b−2,c=1+6​(b+b−1)2.q=e^{i\pi b^{2}},\qquad\widetilde{q}=e^{i\pi b^{-2}},\qquad c=1+6(b+b^{-1})^{2}. (5.19)

The fusion and braiding kernels of Virasoro conformal blocks can be interpreted in terms of Clebsch–Gordan coefficients and 6​j6j-symbols of this modular-double quantum group [math/0007097, 1202.4698, 2309.11540]. Therefore the algebra of topological line operators and the Moore–Seiberg moves of Virasoro T(Q)FT should admit a quantum-group description. From this point of view, the desired topological boundary conditions may be formulated as suitable module categories, or commutative algebra objects, for the representation category of the modular double. The measure d​μ​(L)d\mu(L) should then be related to the Plancherel measure [math/0007097, 1202.4698] or Haar-type measure naturally associated with this non-compact quantum group.

Again, we emphasize that this should be taken only as a very sloppy proposal, not as a promising construction. At generic qq, the relevant representation theory is continuous and non-compact, so one should not expect a finite groupoid sum over Lagrangian algebras. At special rational or root-of-unity points, finite quantum-group categories may give controlled toy models, closer to ordinary Reshetikhin–Turaev [Reshetikhin:1991tc] or Turaev–Viro theory [Turaev:1992hq]. The generic Virasoro case, however, should involve a genuinely continuous topological-boundary average, more like the Narain integral than like the finite Marolf–Maxfield groupoid sum.

The conceptual payoff would be significant. Pure AdS3\mathrm{AdS}_{3} gravity would then fit the same pattern as the examples studied in this paper: For Narain theories, the topological boundary condition is equivalent to a charge lattice. For the Marolf–Maxfield model, it is a finite set. For pure 3D gravity, it should be the full Virasoro CFT data selected by a topological boundary condition of the doubled Virasoro T(Q)FT. Making this statement precise would require a classification of admissible Virasoro topological boundaries, a derivation of the corresponding measure, and a proof that this topological-boundary average reproduces the known Virasoro-T(Q)FT gravity amplitudes. These are open problems, but the analogy suggests a concrete route for organizing them.

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.
